content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 9,
1992
David Bengston
Agricultural Commissioner
County of Mendocino
Department of Agriculture
Courthouse
Ukiab,
CA
95482
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-333
Dear Commissioner Bengston:
This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on May 26,
1992,
concerning your
duties as Agricultural Commissioner for Mendocino County pursuant
to the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the llActH).l
This letter confirms that your letter dated
June 1,
1992,
accurately summarizes the telephone advice
I
provided to you on May 26,
1992.2
In our conversation,
you asked whether you had
a conflict of
interest with respect to
a land use matter submitted by Kay
Management,
a subsidiary of the Omni Foundation.
You stated that
your only connection to the Omni Foundation was that you were
subpoenaed by the District Attorney to testify as an expert
witness in a criminal action against Omni Foundation concerning
a
toxic waste fire.
You testified as an expert witness as to the
type of fire it was,
and whether the fire was pesticide-related.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Please note that this letter is based on the facts presented to
us.
The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
Moreover, this advice is limited to the provisions of the Act.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-92-333
Page
2
As we discussed, for a conflict of interest to exist under
the Act,
you must have an economic interest in a specific decision
which will be foreseeably and materially affected.
Section 87103
provides:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is
a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
As we discussed,
you may also wish to consider the appearence
of bias.
However, the Act does not require disqualification based
on appearances absent a financial interest.


Our File No.
A—92-333
Page
3
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
According to your facts, none of the provisions of Section
87103 apply.
Absent an economic interest in a decision,
the Act
does not restrict your participation.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
SH:JWW:aa
Scott Hallabrin
.ing General Counsel
•ohn
W.
Wallace
isel,
Legal Division
‘
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1O per page.

sections.question: Whether the requestor had a conflict of interest with respect to a land use matter submitted by Kay Management, a subsidiary of the Omni Foundation, given that the requestor was subpoenaed by the District Attorney to testify as an expert witness in a criminal action against Omni Foundation concerning a toxic waste fire, where the requestor testified as an expert witness regarding the type of fire and whether the fire was pesticide-related.
sections.conclusion: According to the facts presented, none of the provisions of Section 87103 apply. Absent an economic interest in a decision, the Act does not restrict your participation.
sections.question_synthetic: Does an Agricultural Commissioner have a conflict of interest in deciding a land use matter from a company subsidiary when the commissioner previously testified as an expert witness against the parent company in a criminal proceeding?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act because the commissioner does not have an economic interest in the decision as defined by Section 87103, and therefore participation is not restricted.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard FPPC advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document confirms prior telephone advice. Citation to Government Code Section 87103 and case law included. Minor typographical variations in footer signature lines do not affect content extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the requestor had a conflict of interest with respect to a land use matter submitted by Kay Management, a subsidiary of the Omni Foundation, given that the requestor was subpoenaed by the District Attorney to testify as an expert witness in a criminal action against Omni Foundation concerning a toxic waste fire, where the requestor testified as an expert witness regarding the type of fire and whether the fire was pesticide-related.

CONCLUSION: According to the facts presented, none of the provisions of Section 87103 apply. Absent an economic interest in a decision, the Act does not restrict your participation.

Section 87103 provides: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. embedding.summary: The FPPC confirmed telephone advice provided to Mendocino County Agricultural Commissioner David Bengston regarding whether he had a conflict of interest in deciding a land use matter involving a subsidiary of the Omni Foundation, after having testified as an expert witness against the Omni Foundation in a criminal proceeding. The FPPC concluded that no conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act because none of the economic interest provisions of Section 87103 apply.
